text: The surgeon performs a difficult laparoscopic cholecystectomy for perforated cholecystitis. The gallbladder, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct are visible. Blunt dissection is used with a suction irrigator to identify anatomical structures. The cystic duct is exposed, and the Maryland dissector is used to free it from the gallbladder's infundibulum. The remnant of the cystic artery is dissected around, starting laterally and moving medially.

The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to dissect the gallbladder from the liver's edge, starting at the infundibulum and moving up the sides. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid entering the liver bed and cystic plate, navigating through densely adhered tissue due to inflammation. The cystic artery remnant is divided with electrocautery, while the common bile duct and common hepatic duct are visible nearby, requiring cautious dissection to prevent injury.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create space around the cystic duct, placing clips on both the stay and specimen sides. After securing the clips, the cystic duct is divided with endoshears in a single sharp motion. The gallbladder remains densely adhered to surrounding structures, including the cystic plate, requiring careful dissection with electrocautery. The hepatic duct is visible in close proximity, necessitating meticulous tissue separation using the Maryland dissector.

The surgeon continues gentle and methodical dissection of the gallbladder, carefully breaking apart dense tissue adhesions. Despite the tedious nature of the procedure, progress is visible as the gallbladder is gradually freed from surrounding structures.

The surgeon identifies a tubular structure, potentially an accessory duct off the right hepatic duct, and clips it with one clip on the stay side and one on the specimen side before dividing it with endoshears. The aberrant duct is dissected around using a Maryland dissector and is visibly divided.

The surgeon continues careful dissection to fully release the gallbladder from the liver bed, using a suction irrigator for blunt dissection and electrocautery for precise tissue separation. Gentle, small bursts of electrocautery are applied to avoid injury to surrounding structures, progressively mobilizing the gallbladder despite the absence of a clear areolar plane.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder, revealing a large stone embedded within the tissue. Bleeding from the liver bed is controlled using electrocautery. The posterior wall of the gallbladder is left intact to avoid entering the liver bed, while gentle bursts of cautery and counter traction help remove the gallbladder from the liver bed. The liver bed is coagulated to ensure hemostasis.

After the gallbladder is fully removed, the surgeon uses suction irrigation to clear the right upper quadrant for better visualization. The gallbladder is placed into an endo-catch bag. The gallbladder fossa is irrigated, and the intact clips are inspected, confirming no injury to surrounding structures. The surgeon identifies a large gallstone encapsulated in the mesentery, indicating a previous gallbladder perforation, and prepares to remove it from the abdomen.

The surgeon applies gentle bursts of electrocautery on the anterior aspect of the capsule, avoiding the posterior aspect due to uncertainty about underlying structures. The gallstone is placed in an endo-catch bag for removal from the patient's abdomen, completing the operation.
